one_line_takeaway: BAP shows that jointly perturbing image (universal PGD toward affirmative responses) and text (CoT-driven LLM rephrasing) jailbreaks aligned LVLMs far more effectively than vision-only attacks, adding ~29% average ASR and generalizing to bias and adversarial-robustness evaluation.
key_finding: BAP substantially outperforms vision-only baselines. On MiniGPT4 (Table 1), BAP reaches 68.17% average ASR across 13 scenarios vs 37.41% (No Attack), 47.76% (Liu et al., query-dependent) and 44.01% (Qi et al., query-dependent); in query-agnostic settings the baselines collapse (as low as 31.66% avg) while BAP holds at 68.17%. The headline claim is +29.03% ASR improvement on average over baselines, with per-case gains up to +49.30% (query-dependent) and +52.98% (query-agnostic). BAP transfers between open-source models (e.g., MiniGPT4 <-> InstructBLIP high, LLaVA lower due to architecture differences) and jailbreaks commercial models (Gemini, ChatGLM, Qwen, ERNIE Bot), though ~22.50% lower on average than black-box open-source transfer. Beyond jailbreaking, BAP is repurposed to elicit biased responses (+30.34% bias-induction ASR on average) and to serve as an adversarial-robustness attack (over 85% ASR for targeted/untargeted image mis-description).
core_contribution: The paper proposes BAP (Bi-Modal Adversarial Prompt Attack), a framework to jailbreak large vision-language models (LVLMs) by perturbing BOTH the visual and textual modalities simultaneously, rather than only the image as in prior work. BAP has two coupled stages: (1) query-agnostic image perturbing, which embeds a universal adversarial perturbation into the input image via PGD, optimized against a query-agnostic corpus of affirmative prefixes (e.g., 'Sure...') and negative inhibitions so the image nudges the model toward positive responses regardless of the text query's specific harmful intent; and (2) intent-specific text optimization, which fixes the adversarial image and uses an LLM (instantiated with ChatGPT) with Chain-of-Thought reasoning in an initialization/feedback/iteration loop to analyze why a jailbreak failed and rephrase the textual prompt for the specific harmful intent. The stated novelty is exploiting the bi-modal fusion property of aligned LVLMs, which the authors argue single-modality (vision-only) attacks fail to compromise. This is the paper's own contribution, distinct from the cited vision-only baselines Liu et al. and Qi et al.
threat_model: Red-teaming / jailbreak setting. Attacker goal: supply an adversarial (image, text) prompt pair that bypasses the LVLM's safety alignment and forces generation of harmful content (illegal instructions, hate speech, etc.). Attacker capability (white-box, following Shayegani et al. and Qi et al.): access to the target LVLM's architecture and weights/gradients, but NOT to system prompts or training data. Image perturbations are L-infinity bounded: ||x*_v - x_v||_inf <= epsilon (epsilon = 32/255 in experiments). A black-box scenario is also evaluated via transfer (craft on a white-box source model, apply to other models) and against commercial APIs where only input/output access is assumed.
limitations: Authors' stated limitations: (1) visual adversarial prompts require white-box gradients (PGD), limiting effectiveness in true black-box settings; gradient-free construction is left as future work; (2) text optimization is computationally expensive due to repeated LVLM/LLM interactions. Reviewer-noted caveats grounded in the text: commercial black-box results are on a reduced protocol (only 3 scenarios IA/MG/PH and 50 queries per model) and are notably weaker; automatic ASR relies on an LLM judge (P_J) which could bias reported numbers, though manual verification is reported; only 7B-scale open-source LVLMs (LLaVA-V1.5-7B, MiniGPT-4/Vicuna-7B, InstructBLIP/Vicuna-7B) are tested white-box; the corpus and CoT rephrasing strategies (contextual deception, semantic rephrasing) are heuristic. The paper is offensive red-teaming and evaluates no defense beyond noting future defense work.

datasets_used: SafetyBench (Liu et al. [37]) — 13 prohibited scenarios/behaviors based on OpenAI and Meta usage policies (IA, HS, MG, PH, EH, FR, PO, PL, PV, LO, FA, HC, GD)
AdvBench (Zou et al. [39]) — 521 harmful behaviors; deduplicated and merged into SafetyBench scenarios
quantitative_results: MiniGPT4 (Table 1): BAP average ASR 68.17% vs No Attack 37.41%, Liu et al. QD 47.76%, Qi et al. QD 44.01%
Query-agnostic averages: Liu et al. as low as 31.66%, Qi et al. 33.90%, while BAP stays at 68.17%
Reported gains: +29.03% average ASR over baselines; up to +49.30% (query-dependent), up to +52.98% (query-agnostic)
Commercial black-box ASR ~22.50% lower on average than open-source black-box transfer
Bias induction: +30.34% ASR on average after successful jailbreak (scenarios IA, PH, PO)
Adversarial robustness repurposing: over 85% ASR (targeted class = horse; untargeted = 30 animals)
Implementation: PGD 3000 steps, step-size 1, epsilon = 32/255; text iterations N = 5; ChatGPT as the optimizer/judge LLM M; NVIDIA A800 cluster
baselines_compared: No Attack (raw harmful queries fed directly to the LVLM)
Liu et al. [37] — typographic + intent-related images as visual adversarial prompts (vision-only)
Qi et al. [49] — visual adversarial prompt optimized on a scenario-specific harmful corpus (vision-only)
